edges.explanation: Backlog projected to 113,000 even with record investment proves current trajectory cannot clear it
Record investment failing to reduce the backlog proves the system was defunded below the level that can self-recover
Backlog growing despite record spending proves the funding gap is real and unacknowledged by parties
20% on remand from backlogs and 48% reoffending proves the system produces failure at every stage — more prisons don't fix this
The remand-overcrowding-early release-reoffending cascade is the vicious cycle the defunding claim describes
48% reoffending from short sentences directly justifies the presumption against sentences under 12 months
£1bn/yr cut causing self-representation, unresolved disputes, and unchallenged evictions proves legal aid is infrastructure not luxury
The LASPO damage directly sizes and justifies the restoration policy
Legal aid cuts are one of the specific austerity defunding mechanisms that created the system failure
Prison population doubling from longer sentences not more crime proves it's a demand problem driven by sentencing not crime levels
Crime at historic lows while imprisonment doubled proves tougher sentences have not reduced crime
25 years of Portuguese evidence with HIV down 95% and crime down proves decriminalisation outperforms punitive approaches
Portuguese success and £4 per £1 treatment return directly justify the drug policy commission and decriminalisation
Certainty of detection mattering more than severity is the primary criminological evidence against mandatory minimum sentences
Detection over severity directly supports community sentences and swift justice over long incarceration
42% longer case times since 2010 with every stage slower proves the system cannot recover on its current trajectory
Every stage being slower directly justifies the sustained court investment and Institute for Justice
Prisons being a symptom of sentencing policy directly supports sentencing reform as the causal intervention
Tougher sentences not reducing crime proves the system's punitive orientation is counterproductive and part of the failure cycle
Legal aid being infrastructure means its defunding is part of the systemic collapse the vicious cycle describes
Punitive drug policy being evidence-free while reform is evidence-based directly supports the commission and decriminalisation
Current investment being insufficient proves parties have not stated how they would fund recovery
Court investment depends on the claim that the system was defunded and the vicious cycle can only be broken by sustained spending
Sentencing reform depends on the claim that the demand side (longer sentences) drives the vicious cycle not crime levels
Legal aid restoration depends on the claim that its defunding is a root cause of system failure
Drug policy reform depends on the finding that current enforcement spending is wasteful and treatment gives better returns
Tougher sentences and more prisons directly contradict the evidence that sentencing reform not capacity is the solution
Court estate investment aligns with the direction if insufficient in scale and not paired with systemic reform
Sentencing review and specialist courts align but no commitment to fund court or legal aid recovery undermines delivery
Sentencing review and public health approach to youth violence align with evidence-based direction
Ending overcrowding, through-the-gate support, and prison officer recruitment directly match the evidence-based position
Youth diversion, secure schools, and Women's Justice Board represent comprehensive sentencing and rehabilitation reform
Right to affordable legal assistance creates the statutory framework for legal aid restoration
£2.5bn court repair and judicial recruitment exceed the evidence-based minimum and match the direction
£11bn legal aid restoration is the most ambitious and exceeds the phased restoration policy
Drug law reform toward regulated market matches the evidence-based decriminalisation direction
Mandatory jail for all violent crime and knife possession would massively increase prison population without evidence of crime reduction
Mandatory life for drug dealing contradicts 25 years of Portuguese evidence that decriminalisation reduces harm and crime
Crime at historic lows while imprisonment doubled directly contradicts the premise that the system needs more punishment
Court capacity enables sentencing reform to work because reformed sentences require functioning courts to deliver swift justice
Reducing short sentence demand frees court and prison capacity making the court investment more effective

cross_domain_edges.explanation: Justice needing £3-4bn/yr additional is another component of the total unfunded gap across all domains
Court backlog directly affects asylum case processing times creating the detention and delay problems the immigration domain identifies
Legal aid cuts for housing eviction defence connect directly to the housing domain's finding that insecurity drives poor health and welfare outcomes
Prison health including mental health and addiction treatment depends on NHS provision linking justice failure to health system pressure
Post-release unemployment and homelessness feed into the welfare system making justice failure a driver of welfare dependency
